sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**Cerebrovascular Accident (Stroke)**
A stroke is a sudden neurological deficit caused by interruption of blood flow to the brain (ischemic stroke) or bleeding into the brain (haemorrhagic stroke). It is a medical emergency.

**Transient Ischemic Attack (TIA)**
A TIA is a temporary episode of neurological dysfunction caused by a brief interruption of blood flow to the brain, without permanent brain injury. Symptoms resolve within 24 hours, often within minutes. TIAs are a significant risk factor for future stroke.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Sudden onset of neurological symptoms, which may include:
  - Weakness, numbness or paralysis of the face (facial droop, asymmetry)
  - Arm or leg weakness or numbness (especially on one side of the body)
  - Speech difficulties (slurred speech, difficulty finding words, or inability to speak)
  - Vision changes (sudden loss of vision, double vision, or visual field deficit)
  - Balance or coordination problems (dizziness, vertigo, or ataxia)
  - Severe headache (especially sudden and severe, which may suggest haemorrhagic stroke)
  - Dysphagia, difficulty in swallowing
  - Altered level of consciousness (most common in haemorrhagic stroke due to increased ICP and brainstem involvement)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Stroke mimics such as hypoglycaemia, seizure with post-ictal deficit, intracranial lesion, migraine, electrolyte disturbance, sepsis, syncope, vertigo syndromes, or functional neurological disorder must be considered
- A resolving deficit does not exclude serious pathology; patients with probable TIA still require urgent hospital assessment
- Establishing the exact last-known-well time is critical for reperfusion and stroke-centre decisions

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Administer oxygen only if hypoxaemic or in respiratory distress
- Position to protect the airway and optimise comfort without delaying transfer
- Record a full vital sign survey, especially blood glucose and conscious state
- Establish and document the exact last-known-well time
- Conduct FAST or BE-FAST assessment and document focal deficits clearly (BE-FAST: B = Balance — sudden loss of balance or coordination; E = Eyes — sudden vision changes or double vision; F = Face drooping; A = Arm weakness; S = Speech difficulty; T = Time to call for help)
- If the history suggests TIA or posterior circulation stroke, keep the case time critical even if FAST is negative
- Consider RACE score as an adjunct to identify possible large vessel occlusion
- Pre-notify the receiving service early and prioritise transport to an appropriate stroke-capable centre

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Apply cardiac monitoring and obtain a 12 lead ECG where this does not delay transport
- Consider advanced airway support only if airway reflexes or ventilation are failing
- Gain IV access if clinically indicated for resuscitation, seizure management, or hospital readiness
- Consider point-of-care blood testing only if it informs immediate management without delaying transfer
- Consider IV fluids only if clinically indicated and avoid unnecessary glucose-containing fluids unless hypoglycaemia is present
- Manage seizures, nausea, or pain only where clinically necessary and without slowing stroke transport

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Maintain a high index of suspicion for stroke in patients with new focal neurological symptoms once obvious mimics such as hypoglycaemia have been excluded
- Patients should have continuous limb lead cardiac monitoring where feasible to capture arrhythmias, especially AF, but transport should not be delayed to obtain a 12 lead ECG unless there is a specific indication such as concurrent chest pain
- The timing of symptom onset or last-known-well time must be clearly defined so suitability for reperfusion therapy can be assessed by the receiving stroke service
- Do not administer aspirin in the prehospital setting — haemorrhagic stroke cannot be excluded without CT imaging. Aspirin is initiated by the hospital after stroke type is confirmed
